transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Riley. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi. Thanks for having me. I guess I’ve been thinking a lot about my recent experiences at the convention. It was... overwhelming, to say the least. I felt like I was constantly trying to meet everyone’s expectations while also dealing with my own stress. It’s just hard to balance everything, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like the convention was quite a challenging experience for you. You were navigating not only the expectations of others but also your own stress, which can be really tough. Can you tell me more about what specifically felt overwhelming for you during that time?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it was just a lot. I had friends who wanted to do so many things, and I felt this pressure to keep everyone happy. I kept thinking, “How can I satisfy everyone’s needs and still take care of myself?” It felt like I was going to end up drained and not enjoy anything. I ended up sending an email to set some boundaries, but even that made me feel frustrated and worried about how they might react. It’s like I wanted to be there for them, but I also didn’t want to feel overwhelmed.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you were really caught in a tough spot, wanting to support your friends while also feeling the weight of your own needs. Setting boundaries can be so challenging, especially when you care about how others might react. I can sense that frustration and worry you felt after sending the email. What was it like for you to take that step and communicate your boundaries?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Taking that step felt... complicated. On one hand, I knew it was necessary to protect my own energy, but on the other hand, I felt guilty for even needing to set those boundaries. I worried that my friends might think I was being clingy or not wanting to spend time with them. It’s like I was trying to do the right thing, but it just added to my stress. I wanted to enjoy the convention, but I kept feeling this nagging doubt about whether I was being fair to everyone.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It seems like you were really torn between your own needs and the desire to be there for your friends. The guilt you felt about setting boundaries is understandable, especially when you care about how others perceive you. It’s like you were trying to do what felt right for you, but that came with its own set of worries and doubts. How did you manage those feelings of guilt and doubt while you were at the convention?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Honestly, I didn’t manage them very well. I kept replaying the situation in my head, thinking about whether I should have done things differently. I felt sad and lonely at times, even when I was surrounded by friends. I tried to distract myself by focusing on the activities, but that didn’t really help. I just kept feeling this weight of expectation, both from my friends and myself. It was like I was stuck in this loop of frustration and worry, and it made it hard to really enjoy anything.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds really tough, Riley. Even in the midst of friends and activities, you felt a deep sense of sadness and loneliness, which can be so isolating. It’s understandable that trying to distract yourself didn’t ease that weight of expectation. It seems like you were caught in a cycle of frustration and worry, which made it difficult to be present and enjoy the moment. What do you think might help you break that cycle in the future, or is there something you wish you could have done differently during the convention?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’ve been thinking about that. Maybe if I had been more upfront with my friends about how I was feeling, it could have helped. But then again, I worry about how they might react. I guess I need to find a way to communicate better without feeling so guilty. It might help to remind myself that it’s okay to prioritize my own needs sometimes. I’m not sure how to do that yet, but I think it’s something I need to work on. Just figuring out how to balance everything without feeling overwhelmed would be a start.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re starting to recognize the importance of being more upfront with your friends about your feelings, which is a valuable insight. It’s understandable to worry about their reactions, but it seems like you’re also realizing that prioritizing your own needs is essential for your well-being. Finding that balance can be challenging, but it’s great that you’re open to working on it. What do you think might be a small step you could take towards communicating your needs more effectively in the future?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe I could start by having a more casual conversation with them before events, just to gauge how everyone is feeling and share a bit about my own needs. It doesn’t have to be a big deal, just something light to open the door for honest communication. I think if I approach it that way, it might feel less daunting. Also, I could practice reminding myself that it’s okay to say no sometimes without feeling guilty. Just taking small steps could help me feel more comfortable with it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful approach, Riley. Starting with a casual conversation could create a more relaxed atmosphere for sharing your feelings and needs, which might make it easier for everyone to open up. It’s also great that you’re considering practicing self-compassion and allowing yourself to say no without guilt. Taking those small steps can really help build your confidence over time. How do you feel about trying out this approach the next time you’re in a similar situation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful about it, actually. Just thinking about having that casual conversation makes it seem less intimidating. I know it won’t be easy, but if I can remind myself that it’s okay to express my needs, maybe it won’t feel so overwhelming. I guess I just need to take it one step at a time and see how it goes. It’s nice to think that I could have a more enjoyable experience next time, rather than getting caught up in all the stress.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful about it, Riley. Just the idea of having a casual conversation can make a big difference in how you approach future situations. Taking it one step at a time sounds like a wise plan, and it’s encouraging that you’re focusing on the possibility of a more enjoyable experience. As you move forward, what do you think might help you stay grounded and remind yourself of this newfound perspective when those feelings of stress start to creep in?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think having some kind of reminder for myself could really help. Maybe I could write down a few affirmations or key points about prioritizing my needs and keeping communication open. I could keep it somewhere visible, like on my phone or a sticky note on my desk. Also, taking a moment to breathe and check in with myself when I start feeling overwhelmed might be useful. Just pausing to acknowledge my feelings could help me stay grounded and remember that it’s okay to take care of myself.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like excellent strategies, Riley. Writing down affirmations or key points can serve as a powerful reminder of your commitment to prioritizing your needs and fostering open communication. Keeping them visible is a great idea, too. Taking a moment to breathe and check in with yourself when you start to feel overwhelmed can really help you stay connected to your feelings and needs. It’s about creating those small moments of self-care that can make a big difference. How do you feel about implementing these reminders and practices in your daily routine?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel pretty good about it, actually. Having those reminders could help me stay focused on what I want to work on, and it feels manageable. I think incorporating those small moments of self-care into my routine could really help me feel more balanced. It’s nice to think that I can take control of my feelings a bit more instead of letting them overwhelm me. I’m looking forward to trying it out and seeing how it goes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m glad to hear that you feel good about it, Riley. It sounds like you’re finding a sense of empowerment in taking control of your feelings and creating a more balanced routine for yourself. Looking forward to trying out these strategies is a positive step, and it’s great that you’re open to seeing how it unfolds. As you start to incorporate these reminders and moments of self-care, what do you think will be the most important thing for you to keep in mind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the most important thing for me to keep in mind is to be patient with myself. Change doesn’t happen overnight, and it’s okay if I stumble along the way. I need to remind myself that it’s a process and that it’s okay to take small steps. Also, I want to remember that prioritizing my own needs doesn’t mean I’m being selfish; it’s just part of taking care of myself so I can be there for others too. Keeping that perspective will help me stay grounded as I work on these changes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful perspective, Riley. Being patient with yourself and recognizing that change is a process can really help ease the pressure you might feel. It’s so important to remember that prioritizing your needs is not selfish; it’s a way to ensure you can be your best self for both you and those around you. Keeping that mindset will likely serve you well as you navigate these changes. How do you feel about checking in with yourself regularly to see how you’re doing with this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in with myself regularly sounds like a good idea. It could help me stay aware of how I’m feeling and whether I’m sticking to my reminders and practices. Maybe I could set aside a few minutes each week to reflect on how things are going and adjust if I need to. It feels reassuring to think that I can create that space for myself to evaluate my progress and be gentle with myself along the way. I think it’ll help me stay on track and feel more connected to my own needs.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful plan, Riley. Setting aside time each week to reflect on your feelings and progress can create a valuable space for self-awareness and adjustment. It’s great that you’re focusing on being gentle with yourself during this process. This kind of intentional check-in can help you stay connected to your needs and reinforce the positive changes you’re working towards. How do you envision your first check-in going? What do you hope to focus on during that time?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I envision my first check-in being a quiet moment where I can really reflect without distractions. I’d like to focus on how I’ve been feeling since I started implementing the reminders and self-care practices. I want to see if I’ve been able to communicate my needs better and how that’s affected my interactions with friends. I hope to also acknowledge any challenges I faced and celebrate any small victories, no matter how minor they seem. Just taking that time to be honest with myself about my progress will be important, I think.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a thoughtful and intentional approach for your first check-in, Riley. Creating a quiet moment for reflection can really help you connect with your feelings and experiences. Focusing on your emotions, communication, and the impact on your interactions with friends will give you valuable insights. Acknowledging both challenges and small victories is a wonderful way to celebrate your progress and stay motivated. It seems like you’re setting yourself up for a meaningful and constructive reflection. How do you feel about the idea of celebrating those small victories, and what might that look like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think celebrating small victories is really important, and it can help me stay motivated. It doesn’t have to be anything big; even just acknowledging that I spoke up about my needs or took a moment for self-care feels like a win. I might treat myself to something simple, like enjoying a favorite snack or taking a little time to do something I love, like reading or watching a show. I think it’s about recognizing those moments and giving myself credit for the progress I’m making, no matter how small. It could really help reinforce the positive changes I’m trying to make.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a lovely way to celebrate your small victories, Riley. Recognizing and honoring those moments, no matter how minor they may seem, can really reinforce your progress and keep you motivated. Treating yourself to something enjoyable, like a favorite snack or some time for a hobby, sounds like a wonderful way to acknowledge your efforts. It’s about giving yourself the credit you deserve for taking those steps toward positive change. How do you feel about integrating this practice of celebration into your routine moving forward?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel really positive about integrating this practice into my routine. It seems like a nice way to create a more supportive environment for myself as I work on these changes. Just having that little moment of celebration can remind me that I’m making progress, even when things feel tough. I think it’ll help me stay motivated and reinforce the idea that taking care of myself is important. I’m looking forward to finding those moments to celebrate, and I think it’ll make the journey feel a bit lighter.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s wonderful to hear that you feel positive about integrating this practice into your routine, Riley. Creating a supportive environment for yourself is so important, and those moments of celebration can truly help lighten the journey. It sounds like you’re building a strong foundation for your self-care and personal growth. As you move forward, what are you most excited about in this process of prioritizing your needs and celebrating your progress?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m most excited about the possibility of feeling more balanced and in control of my life. I think prioritizing my needs will help me enjoy my time with friends more, rather than feeling overwhelmed. I’m looking forward to seeing how these changes can improve my overall well-being and how I interact with others. Celebrating my progress, no matter how small, feels like it will help me cultivate a more positive mindset. I’m hopeful that this journey will lead to a deeper sense of fulfillment and connection, both with myself and with the people I care about.
